program: Business English — Executive Communication for International Leadership
hub_subtitle: Business English Executivo para Reuniões Internacionais e Liderança

lesson.menu_desc: Talking about progress and results with the present perfect -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 4 -- The Story So Far -- Talking About Progress with the Present Perfect
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Izabel Cristina de Pieri | Aula 4 -- The Story So Far

listenings.text: Hi Izabel, it's Tom from the board. I just wanted to say how impressed we have all been with the progress this year. When we first invested, the company was tiny and everything ran on spreadsheets. Since then, you have expanded the team, you have reached two new markets, and you have broken even on the new product line. You have not automated the reporting yet, and that is completely fine, because these things take time. Personally, I think you have grown the most of anyone on this call, especially in English. Keep it up, and we will talk on Thursday.

Good morning, everyone. This is Nina, with a short progress update before the call. Over the past year, the company has changed a great deal. Headcount has grown from twelve people to twenty, revenue has increased every quarter, and we have reached our main benchmark ahead of schedule. We have invested in new equipment, and we have started to scale up production in Valinhos. We have not solved everything: the reporting is still partly manual, and Izabel still prepares some numbers by hand. But overall, we have made strong progress this year. Izabel will share the full details on Thursday. Thank you, and talk soon.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to put money into something to make it grow
to grow bigger or add more
to arrive at a level, a target, or a place
the number of people a company employs
a standard or target used to compare performance
something done successfully, a result you are proud of
to reach the point where costs equal income

paras: Over the last three years, AVRT has changed a great deal. When Izabel took over as CEO, the company was small and everything ran on spreadsheets. Since then, the team has expanded, revenue has grown every quarter, and the business has reached new markets in North America. Izabel has led every step of that journey, and she has grown with the company.

The progress has not been easy. AVRT has invested heavily in new equipment, and it has hired engineers to scale up production. It has also improved its reporting, although Izabel has not fully automated it yet, so she still prepares some numbers by hand. 'We have come a long way,' she likes to say, 'but we have not finished.'

So far, the results have been strong. The company has broken even on the new product line, and it has hit almost every benchmark the partners set last year. Izabel has just presented these numbers to the board, and for the first time, she has done it entirely in English. 'A year ago, I could not have imagined this,' she admits. 'I have improved more than I expected.'
reading.source: Adapted from an executive profile, The Leadership Brief

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: AVRT has stayed exactly the same for three years.
AVRT has grown and improved a great deal, and Izabel has grown with it, though the work is not finished.
Izabel has decided to leave AVRT and sell the company.

items: AVRT has expanded and reached new markets in North America.
'the team has expanded, revenue has grown every quarter, and the business has reached new markets in North America.'
Izabel has fully automated the reporting.
'she has not fully automated it yet, so she still prepares some numbers by hand.'
The company has broken even on the new product line.
'The company has broken even on the new product line.'
Izabel has never presented to the board in English.
'Izabel has just presented these numbers to the board... entirely in English.'

parts: even on the new line. Izabel
it yet, so she still prepares some numbers by hand.

gapfill.text: Present perfect = has / have + past participle (has changed, has reached, has broken). Use it for a change or result up to now, with 'since 2023', 'for three years', 'so far', 'yet', 'just', 'already'. Use the simple past for a finished time: 'She presented yesterday.'

bank.label: Useful language for talking about progress
items: We have made strong progress on...
We have already... but we have not... yet.
Compared to 2023, headcount has grown from... to...
We have just broken even on...
We have come a long way, but there is still work to do.

lf.text: Use the present perfect (has/have + participle) for a change up to now, with 'since', 'so far', 'just', 'yet' (A, C). Use the simple past for a finished time such as 'last quarter' or 'a year ago' (B, D).

items: Tell the board how far AVRT has come this year. Use the present perfect: what the team has expanded, reached, and improved.
A partner asks if you have broken even yet. Answer, then say what you have not finished yet.
Compare then and now: 'A year ago we... Now we have...' (simple past vs present perfect).
Describe your own progress in English since you started this program, using 'so far' and 'since'.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = has changed, 2 = has expanded, 3 = has reached, 4 = has broken, 5 = has not automated.
Progress (present perfect): 'Since January, the team has expanded, revenue has grown, and we have reached two new markets.'
Then vs now: 'A year ago, I prepared everything by hand. Now we have improved the reporting a lot.'
Not finished: 'We have not automated the reporting yet, but we have already broken even.'
answerkey.note: Present perfect = has/have + past participle. 'Since' + a start point (since January); 'for' + a length of time (for three years).
